mechanism: Participants read descriptions of behaviors in either imperfective or perfective aspect, then rated intentionality and demonstrated conceptual accessibility via reaction-time or naming task. A between-subjects comparison tests whether aspect modulates intention attribution through accessibility of action-related concepts. Mediation analysis quantifies the indirect effect of aspect on intentionality through imagined-action richness, using bootstrapped confidence intervals or product-of-coefficients method.

Observers are shown displays of items with measurable properties (size, color, etc.). Memory is tested via change-detection or recall task. Individual remembered values are compared to ensemble statistics (mean size by color, global mean) to quantify bias toward higher-order summary statistics. Analysis partitions remembered deviations into components attributable to individual-item variance and ensemble-statistic bias via regression or mixed-effects modeling.

Participants consume a sensory stimulus (sweet, bitter, or neutral) in a within-subjects or between-subjects design. They then evaluate written descriptions of moral scenarios on ordinal scales. Mean moral-judgment ratings are compared across taste condition via ANOVA or mixed-effects model. Moderation analysis tests whether political ideology amplifies or dampens the taste-judgment association.

Participants receive an environmental or priming manipulation (cleansing reminder vs. control) in a between-subjects design. They then provide self-reported political attitudes and moral judgments of written scenarios on ordinal scales. Outcome distributions are compared via chi-squared test (categorical political identity) or ANOVA (ordinal/continuous judgment scales). Group differences quantify the effect of the priming manipulation.

Participants in each cultural group receive a mortality-salience prime or control prime in a between-subjects factorial design. They then respond to written vignettes (person-norm violation or victim scenario) with ratings or judgments on scales. Group-by-condition ANOVAs or mixed-effects models partition variance by cultural group, prime, and their interaction to test whether mortality salience modulates outcome differently across cultures.

Participants are exposed to a visual prime (flag image vs. control) in a between-subjects design. Political attitudes, implicit associations, voting intentions, and voting behavior are measured at multiple time points (baseline, immediate, long-term follow-up). Treatment-control contrasts are computed via t-tests or regression at each time point; effect persistence is characterized via temporal decay curves or survival of statistical significance.

Participants are exposed to stimuli varying in emotional intensity (images, unpredictable stimulation) and choose between two regulation strategies (engagement vs. disengagement) in a within-subjects or mixed design. Choice proportions are aggregated by intensity level and tested via binomial tests or logistic regression, where intensity modulates the log-odds of strategy selection.

Participants read descriptions of a leader displaying either dominant or communal behavior, crossed with leader race-gender combinations (race x gender factorial design, between-subjects). Participants rate leader status, competence, and likeability on scales. ANOVAs or regression models partition variance by race, gender, behavior, and their interactions to test moderation of the dominance-backlash effect by race and gender.
domain: leadership and intersectionality in social perception

mechanism: Sociometric status (respect within groups) and socioeconomic status are measured via self-report or peer-rating surveys. Subjective well-being is measured via rating scales (life satisfaction, emotion frequency). Correlational studies compute Pearson or Spearman correlations between status and well-being. Longitudinal analysis regresses well-being change on status change; experimental studies manipulate status-group assignment and measure well-being outcomes. Mediation analysis quantifies indirect effects through power and social acceptance.

Participants receive a choice-concept prime or control condition (between-subjects), then respond to statements about wealth inequality, societal factors, and policy via rating scales. Outcomes (continuous or ordinal) are compared between treatment and control via t-tests or Mann-Whitney tests. Moderation analysis tests whether the effect of the prime on belief in personal agency depends on measured or manipulated factors. Mediation analysis decomposes the path from prime to policy attitudes through agency attribution.

Stimuli showing persons (upright or inverted) are presented in a recognition task. Response accuracy is measured and compared between upright and inverted conditions via repeated-measures ANOVA or logistic regression. Inversion effect (decrement in upright relative to inverted performance) is computed within each gender condition. Sex of stimulus is tested as a moderator of the inversion effect via interaction terms.

Participants rate environmental issues on moral-concern scales (harm, care, purity, fairness, etc.), and separately report environmental attitudes (pro-environment vs. anti-environment ratings). Mediation analysis tests whether moral values mediate the link between political ideology and environmental attitudes. Moderation analysis tests whether reframing rhetoric in moral dimensions (purity vs. harm/care) shifts the ideology-attitude association. Content analysis of text data counts moral-concept occurrences and relates prevalence to source type.

Older and younger adults complete a memory task or control task in a within-subjects or mixed design. Before and after task completion, subjective age is collected via rating scale. Change in subjective age (post-pre) is compared between age groups and task types via ANOVA or mixed-effects regression, with baseline age group and task type as factors. Effect specificity is tested by contrasting memory tasks with non-memory control tasks.

Participants view advertisements combining product price (expensive vs. cheap, between-subjects) with imagery type (sexual vs. neutral, within-subjects). Likeability or distaste ratings are collected on scales. A 2x2 ANOVA or mixed-effects model partitions variance by price, imagery, and their interaction. Gender moderates the price-imagery interaction; separate models fit men vs. women to quantify group-level differences in sensitivity to the price-imagery pairing.

Racial-progress information (chronic salience or experimental prime) is presented or withheld in a between-subjects design. Participants report perceptions of anti-White discrimination on rating scales. Moderation analysis tests whether the effect of progress salience on discrimination perception depends on belief in status-hierarchy legitimacy (measured as continuous moderator). Mediation analysis quantifies the role of threat perception; self-affirmation is manipulated to test whether affirmation eliminates the threat-mediated effect.

Information about racial demographic shifts (majority-minority future, salient vs. control condition) is presented in a between-subjects design. Participants report political-party leaning, political ideology, and policy attitudes on rating or choice scales. Treatment-control differences are tested via t-tests, chi-squared tests, or logistic/ordinal regression. Moderation analysis examines whether group-status threat (measured via threat-perception scale) moderates the prime-outcome association. Mediation analysis quantifies the indirect effect of the prime on politics through threat perception.

Free-will beliefs are measured via survey or manipulated via experimental exposure (neuroscience information vs. control). Punishment attitudes are measured on rating scales (retributive vs. consequentialist dimensions). Correlational studies compute associations between belief and attitude; experimental studies compare treatment-control outcomes via t-tests or ANCOVA. Moderation analysis examines whether the free-will-belief effect on punishment depends on measured personal characteristics or experimental framing.

Speakers are assigned to high or low rank (between-subjects), and their voices are recorded and analyzed for acoustic features (pitch, loudness, pitch variability, loudness variability) using signal-processing algorithms to extract continuous measurements. A second group of perceivers listens to voice samples and judges speaker rank. Regression models relate objective acoustic cues to speaker rank; discriminant or logistic analysis quantifies how well perceivers infer rank from acoustic cues alone. Accuracy metrics compare perceptual judgments to ground-truth rank.

Negotiators are assigned to one of three conditions (strong alternatives, weak alternatives, no alternatives, between-subjects). Negotiation outcomes (first offer, final agreement price) are measured. Treatment-control comparisons via ANOVA or t-test quantify differences in first offers and final outcomes. Mediation analysis tests whether the effect of alternative strength on outcomes is mediated by anchoring (measured via perception of first offers or explicit anchor ratings). Moderation analysis examines whether effects change under countervailing-anchor conditions.

Participants receive a prime (God-concept cue vs. control, between-subjects) and then choose whether to engage in a risky option described in narrative form (nonmoral domain: gambling, adventure sports, etc.). Risk-taking choice proportions are compared between prime conditions via chi-squared test or logistic regression. Perceived danger and perceived-safety-from-God are measured and tested as mediators via indirect-effect analysis. Moderation by religious orientation (continuous) is tested by interaction terms. Outcome valence (success vs. failure of the risk) is manipulated to measure subsequent attitudes via post-hoc surveys.
